title: Training Recursive Models - A Frontier in Adaptive Compute
description:
Github Repo (see `recursive` branch): https://github.com/TrelisResearch/nanochat
One-click Runpod Template: https://console.runpod.io/deploy?template=ikas3s2cii&ref=jmfkcdio
Original Karpathy Repo: https://github.com/karpathy/nanochat
Wandb logs: https://wandb.ai/trelis/nanochat
Model repo: https://huggingface.co/Trelis/nanochat-recursive

References:
- Recursion & Adaptive Compute: https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.05171
- TRM Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04871

Tip: If you subscribe here on YouTube, click the bell to be notified of new vids

Learn more about Trelis ADVANCED-fine-tuning repo: https://trelis.com/advanced-fine-tuning

💡 Done-for-you Custom Fine-tuning Services
Learn More: https://trelis.com/fine-tuning-services/

💸 Starting a New Project/Venture?
Apply for a Trelis Grant: https://trelis.com/trelis-ai-grants/

📧 Get Trelis AI Tutorials by Email
Subscribe on Substack: https://trelis.substack.com

TIMESTAMPS:
0:00 Recursive Nanochat - a comparison with Karpathy’s 500M parameter model
3:07 Benchmark Results on Recursive Nanochat
5:54 What are the benefits of recursive models?
6:52 Recursive Models allow inference on smaller devices and fewer GPUs
8:07 Recursive Models open a pathway to adaptive compute
9:39 Recursive vs Non-recursive Architecture
13:48 How to handle the recursive stream via an adapter
16:49 Training for adaptive compute / recursions - Poisson log-normal recursion sampling
18:14 Handling torch.compile with recursive models
20:07 Implementing adaptive compute (stopping recursions early)
22:14 kv cache strategies for recursive models
24:16 Inference engine (vLLM) implications for recursive models
26:20 Training dynamics of recursive models (Wandb overview), incl. flops utilisation
31:21 Code Review of Trelis/nanochat
32:05 Truncated backpropagation through time
33:56 Recursive loop adapter initialisation
35:25 Dynamic torch compile
37:06 Wrap up
full_text: By using recursion within transformer models, you can build a model that performs almost as well, but allows you to have fewer parameters, which means less VRAMm and potentially allows you to do dynamic compute without having a long series of thinking tokens. This is a concept that has been explored in smaller models. It's been explored in the arc prize in the TRM paper. It's also been explored at the Lama size paper with the Hugan type models, but it has not really been scaled to full size. There's also limited comparison between typical transformers without recursion and then recursion versions. So I thought it would be interesting to take a well-known architecture and approach nanohat from karpathy which is a small 20 layer model and replace 12 of those layers with four recursive layers and see if I train it for the same number of flops whether I get the same better or worse performance. So that's what I've done and I'm going to show you this model that I've made public. I'm going to show you the repo. I'll go through some of the changes I've made there and I'll go through some of the theory about what recursive models might offer as a meaningful improvement to how transformers are designed today. I'm curious whether the large language labs are already using aspects of recursion cuz I can see some strong benefits. There are also some complexities around things like doing torch compile for training and also running continuous batching during inference that I'll talk a little bit about. Now, for background, this is all based on the Karpathi nano chat repo, which you can find on GitHub. It allows you to train a model for about $100 that can respond pretty well to you in English. It's able to score, I mean, roughly in the vicinity of GPT2. The model is quite small. It's about a 500 million parameter model. And the comparative model I will train is 300 uh million parameters. So, a bit more than half the size. I trained and I made a video previously on nano chat and if you like you can find a copy of that. I'll show you here. Just go to hugging face trellis and nano chat and you'll find all of the files and you'll also find my fork of Karpathi's GitHub repo which explains how to train and spin it up quickly using runpod. To advance on that, I took the very same model. I trained on the same amount of data and using the same number of flops, but instead of having 20 layers, I took out 12 of the middle layers and replaced them with just four layers. Yeah, I said um 12 earlier, but it's 16 cuz it's I have two prelude layers, I have four layers in the middle that are recursed. And during training, I will recurse them for an average of four times. So 2 + 4 * 4 is 16 + 2 is 20. So I have the same number of effective layers with the mean recursion. And I'll talk about exactly how the number of recursions is decided during training and during inference. And I completed the exact same pipeline, the same pre-training, the same mid-training and the same post-raining as the original nano chat model. And here are the results. So you can see this chat core metric which is a a mixture of some of the following metrics. uh arc easy, arc challenge, mmlu, gsm8k, human eval spelling b and the core metric here if you look at the original model scores about 27 whereas the recursive model uh scores a little bit lower than that. So when you train for the same number of flops in data you basically get quite similar performance and you can see as you vary at inference time the number of recursions on those middle four layers you get you know maybe a small increase here. Now I'm not showing error bars because I didn't rerun this experiment. Uh it costs about 100 every time you want to run it. So you can say that probably the difference here is not all that large and the trend here is probably also not all that large to be statistically significant. But what is meaningful is that you were able to get a model that is close to half the size and is able to perform quite similarly here in performance. It's particularly interesting if you look at metrics benchmarks around more reasoning type capabilities like GSM8K which is grade school maths. This benchmark involves maths problems. And here you see a very big improvement at least going from two to four recurrences around the middle layers in the performance. This is definitely meaningful. We've got 3.5 going all the way up to well very likely meaningful up to about 6% performance by going from two up to the mean training number of recurrences here. Now again I'd need to run this a few times to ensure that this is statistically significant. And with this kind of a small experiment uh and model I'm not sure I would draw too much conclusion from it. But suffice to say, I think there's evidence that training a model with recurrences on an ISO flops basis can lead you to get a very similar performing model with recursion, but a model that is smaller in terms of VRAMm and potentially allows you to do adaptive compute. Something I'll talk about more here. I won't prove that adaptive compute uh is able to work well from a commercial mindset. That would require more. I would need to show you probably more difficult benchmarks. Also would potentially need to train for longer. My performance right now in reasoning benchmarks here is not that high and we didn't do any reinforcement learning or anything like that. Although Karpath's repo does support that. So it's something that could be interesting to do as a further step. What I'm going to do now is go through some of the theory here. I'm going to show you on a little on my iPad. I'm going to sketch out a few things and when we're done with that I will go into the repo and I'll show you some of the lines of code that I changed in order to get this recursive model to work. All right, let's go through some of the theory. I want to start off and talk about the motivation. So why would you even want to do recursion and the short answer is that it allows you to have a smaller model and potentially do adaptive compute which ultimately could save computed inference time and get better performance. Then I want to talk about the architecture. Architecture. I don't think that's correct. Arcture. And then I want to talk about training and I'll maybe talk a bit about um inference and adaptive compute. All right. So motivation, let's write it here. Two reasons why you might want to do recursive models at least. The first one is let me write that in black. Smaller weights. So, a smaller parameter count. And what this means is you can probably fit more easily on say an iPhone or on a Mac, a laptop. It also means you can fit on fewer GPUs if you're doing training at scale. If previously you needed to split a 20 layer model across two GPUs with 10 layers on each, you could now maybe fit it on one GPU. If you have less pipeline parallel, that's called pipeline parallel. Well, you put them in in series or model parallel, then you have fewer pipeline bubble issues. This is um yeah it's kind of a technical thing during atscale training which I don't do a whole lot of but if you have GPUs in parallel what you end up with is GPUs waiting on previous GPUs and this leads to a bubble that propagates through the pipeline and um that is going to affect the utilization of your GPUs and ultimately increase the training time. So a benefit of having a recursive model is you can fit it on fewer GPUs. The second benefit is it opens up a pathway to adaptive compute. And the idea here is instead of doing a reasoning model where you generate more and more tokens, you instead just do more recursions on the model and you increase the number of recursions if you want to have more reasoning. And this is appealing because you're doing recursion in latent space. If you are generating more tokens, a token is actually just one sample from a probability distribution. And that means when you take that sample, you're actually throwing out information. But if you stay and do the recursions in latent space where you use the full information, that's potentially going to be a richer uh way to do recursion. And also, it allows you to break the link between needing to generate a token to do reasoning. Even if that token is very easy to predict, you still take the same amount of compute in a reasoning model. Whereas if you can increase the number of recursions, there's no tokens being generated. So you you break the link which is constraining between token generation and uh doing more compute. It's kind of a subtle point I might explain on another video at some point. So these are the two reasons. If you don't understand adaptive compute yet, uh we will get to it when we move to the inference section. So don't worry too much. All right. So let's talk about architecture and do that by starting with the standard transformer. So in a transformer I'll draw out what this looks like. You will start off with some inputs. So down here you will have some text input and that text will be broken into subwords or tokens and those tokens are going to go through an embedding layer. So here we will typically have an embedding layer and the embedding layer is an up a lookup table that will take each token maybe out of a vocabulary of 65,000 and for each token it will convert that token into a vector in highdimensional space. So we have now got embeddings or vectors and then these will go through the transformer layers. And here I'm just going to draw three transformer layers. The vectors will propagate through that. They'll be transformed and when they come out the other side, I need to make space here. They will go through basically reverse embeddings. This is often called the LM head or the language model head. And the language model head takes vectors and it converts them into a probability distribution over possible next tokens. So it might say the word 'the' is 20%, quick is 30%, and brown is 40%, or 50%. And you will then sample typically the most likely token uh in order to continue your sequence. So here you have token predictions. Token predictions. That's a typical transformer. And let's write down here typical. Now, what if we want to do recursive? In this case, we're going to do a lot the same. We're going to keep the embeddings layer, but we're now only going to put in a smaller number of intermediate layers. And I should say that each of these layers here consists of attention which allows tokens to take information from previous tokens and also multi-layer perceptrons or feed forward layers. These take a vector representation of a token or a vector representation I should just say let's say 256 dimensions and allows each dimension to pay attention to the other dimensions. So that's the feed forward layer and then the attention allows all of that to pay attention to take information from the previous tokens in the sequence. In the recursive transformer instead of having say three layers here we will just have one layer and rather than just passing through it once we will actually recurse like this and then we will have the LM head. So when I took nano chat and nano chat I should say has got let's talk about nano chat I'll make this one here blue if I look at nano chat nano chat it will have 20 layers so 20 and in the recursive case I actually keep the first two layers that's called prelude then I put in four layers here which are my recursion layers and then I keep two of the last layers. Now I'm pre-training from scratch. So when I say keep, I just mean keep from the base architecture. So recursive in the recursive case I only have eight layers compared to 20. But this middle one here, I will recurse a mean number of four times. So my number of recursions on average will be equal to four. So actually I've got an effective number of layers of 2 * 4 * 4. And so actually not two times, it's 2 plus + 2 = 20. And so that's how I've got 20 effective layers in the recursive and 20 in the original model here. So that's the recursive model. Now, there's a little nuance here around how do you add information back? One way you could add information back is just simply by adding the output back to the new inputs. So if you think of it as um the new input. So the let's call it the mixed input. So if I if I say that what's coming in here is the mixed input. If I say what's coming in here is I and I say here is the recursion. A simple way to do this is you can just say M is equal to I + R and you can just add on a vector basis. So if you have I1, I2, I3, and then you have R1, R2, R3, you could just add them to generate a new vector. That would be M1, M2, M3. And that probably would have worked, but in literature, you'll find another approach, which is uh allowing for a little bit more control. Basically, if I do this, the model has no way to weight the i versus the recurrent stream. It's just adding them. But if you want to weight the recurrent stream, what you can do is you can instead concatenate. So you concatenate such that you have i uh 1, i2, i3, and then you make the vector longer by doing 1 or 2 or 3. And now you have a vector that's twice the width that it needs to be. So you put it through an adapter layer. You put it through a simple linear layer. This is just your adapter. And you adapt it down to half the size to make m1, m2, m3. So this is actually what I do. I concatenate the vectors and then I use an adapter which is trainable. So this is trainable. It's a simple linear layer. So you can think of it just matrices that map down to a smaller dimension. And yeah, this is how I do the mapping for each recurrence. Now on the very first recurrence, these values of or are just zero and you um start off from that. But on the later recurrences, these values will be non zero as you start to build up information in the recurrence. There's another subtle thing here around inference. If you want to boost or if you want to give a kickstart on a given token, instead of starting these ores at zero on the first recurrence, you can use the ore values that you had from the previous token, you can use the the last recurrence or values from the previous token to give a kickstart. Kind of a subtle point, but uh allows you to get some boost in inference. And I am doing that in the code for what it's worth. Okay. Next, I want to talk a bit about um what do I want to talk about? That's the architecture, the training. Okay, so what I described is kind of all you need to know to make the model recursive. You could run Karpath's nano chat without changing much. I did something a little more complicated, which is I'm actually training using different levels of recursion. So the model gets used to seeing different levels of recursion and hopefully can perform better if I lower recursions or raise it during inference. So instead of just using four recurrences, if I draw I'm sampling the recurrences. So I've got here on the x-axis, I've got my number of recurrences and this is during training. And I'm actually doing a plus on log normal. So if you look at the probability here on the y- ais, I'm setting this up so that the mean value is equal to four, which allows me to keep constant the number of layers uh that I'm doing compared to the original. And I'm also capping here. I'm capping at a value of 16, the maximum level of recurrences. And the minimum here is one. You could maybe do zero, but that would be a discontinuous jump for the model. So the minimum number of occurrences is one and the maximum is 16. And this allows the model to sometimes see more recurrences, which hopefully can allow me to better leverage that if I want to do adaptive compute at at inference time. Now there's one issue here with training which is that if you train with a constant number of recurrences there will always be the same pattern of calculations as you run a forward pass through the model but if you vary the number of recurrences there are actually different paths and this is important because torch compile it caches or remembers a given computational path so it can replay it much faster in later training loops it often saves you about 30%. But now that we have 16 possible paths because you can have you can either go through um with one recurrence with two etc up to 16. We've got 16 paths that you need to cache. And this slows down training quite a bit during the first hour. This wouldn't be a big deal if I was training on a lot more data because everything would be compiled. The graphs would be compiled and that would end up a small portion of total training time. But because this is not a very long training, it's only 4 hours. it ends up slowing down training from three hours to I don't know close to double that when I do the recurrent model and you'll see that in the weights and biases curves I show you. So this uh pre-training which maybe was overkill for a first model I should have just kept it constant at four but it does allow you to then probably better vary the number of recurrences. You probably could vary the recurrences anyway even if you only trained it on four. This is um this has been tried on the TRM model for Sudoku and it actually works just increasing the number of recurrences at inference time. It doesn't work on ARC AGI2 although that's harder and I suspect if you pre-train by varying recurrences it probably does help and I think that's what's being borne out when uh training lama or quen type models. So this uh that's my comment on training. Now for inference I just want to talk a little bit about adaptive compute which is kind of a subtle concept and it's got some implications for whether inference would work well or not. But if you look at adaptive compute the idea is well the simplest idea is if you have a hard problem you would do more recursions maybe 16. If you have an easy one do two. The question is how do you know in advance how many recursions you should do? And one approach to it is you could train uh a halting head as the TRM paper does. So you could actually add on an extra head here that kind of taps from the recurrent layers and put on uh a halting head that would predict when the solution or high quality has been reached. This is kind of hard to do though because if you're working with language, it's not obvious what a good quality answer is necessarily. So it can be hard to train a halting head and a simpler proposal and I'll link the papers in the description and I should thank Sean McLeish by the way for discussions around some of these topics but a simple approach is just to look at as you run recursions look at the probability distribution of tokens predicted. So let's say the possible tokens are there quick and brown if you're on a first layer. So let's say recurrence equals 1, you maybe have this kind of a distribution and these all need to add to 100%. If you have or equals 2, so after your second recurrence, maybe you have something like this. And then maybe after your third recurrence, you have something like this. Uh let's say you've now got this. So notice how on that third recurrence, my probability predictions, my distribution is very similar to the second one. Well, you could just determine a rule whereby when my probability distribution looks similar after two subsequent recurrences, that means I can probably stop mathematically. You could look at the KL divergence across the full probability, the full softax, and if the two distributions are very similar, then you say, okay, we'll stop recurring. Now, so this is a simple rule to decide when you should stop uh recurring. Now there's a little bit of complexity because when you stop recurring on a given token, you may find that different tokens have got different optimal amounts of recurrence. And what this leads to, I'll just show kind of somewhat uh graphically although not rigorously. If we look again at the quick and brown, remember that when we do attention, we look back and grab information from earlier tokens. But now the number of recurrences might be different. So we might have some calculations done for the word the for recurrences 1, 2, and three. But for the word the optimal might be just two. And for brown it might be again three. And so when we get to brown typically when we're here we would expect to be looking back at information from the same layer. But this is missing because we've only done two recurrences. So whereas in a physical network with three layers we would always have this value available. In a recurrent network where we stop after two recurrences for quick, we basically have missing information. The solution to this is you just take the latest available state. So that pretty much solves it. And actually whereas in a dense network you typically would store the activate you would typically store um the KV cache for each of the layer each of the layers in a recurrent network you could decide to just throw away the history. So if you recur three times don't bother storing for the first two just always rely on the latest. So you can basically throw out some information uh from the past and only rely on the latest information and this allows you to reduce uh your KV cache and it seems empirically this allows you to keep good performance as well. So you can probably achieve inference in just as simple a way uh when you're running with adaptive compute. Well, when you're running with a recurrent model as with uh a standard model except there is an important uh subtlety here which is that if you recur to different depths at different tokens when you try to do continuous batching with multiple sequences. So this will work straightforwardly with one sequence, but with multiple sequences, you typically in an inference engine like VLM, you will pack together the next token prediction for multiple sequences. And if those different sequences will have different levels of recurrence, they're going to finish forward passing through this network at different points in time. And this poses a problem because you can kind of keep forward passing but then you will have to wait and you will have to idle any of the sequences that have finished recurring while you run the other ones. And this is suboptimal because you won't be using your CUDA cores to the same uh extent as you would have. and basically all of them. It's kind of like you will be it's kind of like you'll be using whichever the max in that sequence needs for recurrence. So if you have one sequence that needs recurrence of 16 but the others only need recurrence of two well the ones that only need two will kind of be idling until you get to 16. And so you need to think about well can you improve upon that?
